inputs.original: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.

inputs.original: Dash to Dock has many of the common “Dock” features you’d expect, including autohide and intellihide, a fixed-width mode, adjustable icon size, and custom themes.

SentenceMap.3: GNOME 3 (which is more commonly used with the GNOME Shell) has an extension framework that lets developers (and users) extend, build on, and shape how the desktop looks, acts and functions.
SentenceMap.4: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.

inputs.original: My biggest pet peeve with GNOME Shell is its legacy app tray that hides in the bottom left of the screen.

SentenceMap.0: Over the past fortnight we asked you to nominate your top extensions for the GNOME desktop.
SentenceMap.2: Having now sifted through the hundreds of entries, we’re ready to reveal your favourite GNOME Shell extensions.
SentenceMap.3: GNOME 3 (which is more commonly used with the GNOME Shell) has an extension framework that lets developers (and users) extend, build on, and shape how the desktop looks, acts and functions.
SentenceMap.4: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.
SentenceMap.5: And just like Plank, Docky or AWN you can add app launchers, rearrange them, and use them to minimise, restore and switch between app windows.
SentenceMap.6: Dash to Dock has many of the common “Dock” features you’d expect, including autohide and intellihide, a fixed-width mode, adjustable icon size, and custom themes.
SentenceMap.7: My biggest pet peeve with GNOME Shell is its legacy app tray that hides in the bottom left of the screen.

SentenceMap.3: GNOME 3 (which is more commonly used with the GNOME Shell) has an extension framework that lets developers (and users) extend, build on, and shape how the desktop looks, acts and functions.
SentenceMap.4: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.

inputs.original: All extraneous non-system applets, indicators and tray icons hide down here.

SentenceMap.2: Having now sifted through the hundreds of entries, we’re ready to reveal your favourite GNOME Shell extensions.
SentenceMap.3: GNOME 3 (which is more commonly used with the GNOME Shell) has an extension framework that lets developers (and users) extend, build on, and shape how the desktop looks, acts and functions.
SentenceMap.4: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.

SentenceMap.0: Over the past fortnight we asked you to nominate your top extensions for the GNOME desktop.
SentenceMap.2: Having now sifted through the hundreds of entries, we’re ready to reveal your favourite GNOME Shell extensions.
SentenceMap.3: GNOME 3 (which is more commonly used with the GNOME Shell) has an extension framework that lets developers (and users) extend, build on, and shape how the desktop looks, acts and functions.
SentenceMap.4: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.
SentenceMap.5: And just like Plank, Docky or AWN you can add app launchers, rearrange them, and use them to minimise, restore and switch between app windows.
SentenceMap.6: Dash to Dock has many of the common “Dock” features you’d expect, including autohide and intellihide, a fixed-width mode, adjustable icon size, and custom themes.
SentenceMap.7: My biggest pet peeve with GNOME Shell is its legacy app tray that hides in the bottom left of the screen.
SentenceMap.8: All extraneous non-system applets, indicators and tray icons hide down here.

inputs.original: This makes it a little harder to use applications that rely on a system tray presence, like Skype, Franz, Telegram, and Dropbox.

SentenceMap.0: Over the past fortnight we asked you to nominate your top extensions for the GNOME desktop.
SentenceMap.2: Having now sifted through the hundreds of entries, we’re ready to reveal your favourite GNOME Shell extensions.
SentenceMap.3: GNOME 3 (which is more commonly used with the GNOME Shell) has an extension framework that lets developers (and users) extend, build on, and shape how the desktop looks, acts and functions.
SentenceMap.4: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.
SentenceMap.5: And just like Plank, Docky or AWN you can add app launchers, rearrange them, and use them to minimise, restore and switch between app windows.
SentenceMap.6: Dash to Dock has many of the common “Dock” features you’d expect, including autohide and intellihide, a fixed-width mode, adjustable icon size, and custom themes.
SentenceMap.7: My biggest pet peeve with GNOME Shell is its legacy app tray that hides in the bottom left of the screen.
SentenceMap.8: All extraneous non-system applets, indicators and tray icons hide down here.
SentenceMap.9: This makes it a little harder to use applications that rely on a system tray presence, like Skype, Franz, Telegram, and Dropbox.

input_modifications.original: This makes it a little harder to use applications that rely on a system tray presence, like Skype, Franz, Telegram, and Dropbox.

SentenceMap.0: Over the past fortnight we asked you to nominate your top extensions for the GNOME desktop.
SentenceMap.2: Having now sifted through the hundreds of entries, we’re ready to reveal your favourite GNOME Shell extensions.
SentenceMap.3: GNOME 3 (which is more commonly used with the GNOME Shell) has an extension framework that lets developers (and users) extend, build on, and shape how the desktop looks, acts and functions.
SentenceMap.4: Dash to Dock takes the GNOME Dash — this is the ‘favourites bar’ that appears on the left-hand side of the screen in the Activities overlay — and transforms it into a desktop dock.
SentenceMap.5: And just like Plank, Docky or AWN you can add app launchers, rearrange them, and use them to minimise, restore and switch between app windows.
SentenceMap.6: Dash to Dock has many of the common “Dock” features you’d expect, including autohide and intellihide, a fixed-width mode, adjustable icon size, and custom themes.
SentenceMap.7: My biggest pet peeve with GNOME Shell is its legacy app tray that hides in the bottom left of the screen.
SentenceMap.8: All extraneous non-system applets, indicators and tray icons hide down here.
SentenceMap.9: This makes it a little harder to use applications that rely on a system tray presence, like Skype, Franz, Telegram, and Dropbox.

inputs.original: TopIcons Plus is the quick way to put GNOME system tray icons back where they belong: on show and in reach.
